subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, precise aim with explicit explanation of relevance to selectiveâattention processes
Solid description of relevant theories (selective attention, automaticity, speedâofâprocessing) and linkage to predicted Stroop effect
Hypotheses state IV and DV clearly and operationalize variables quantitatively
weaknesses: Theoretical explanations, while correct, are somewhat superficial and could be elaborated
Operationalization of timing procedures in the hypothesis lacks precision
Contextual details of the original study (sample size, task specifics) are underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction fully meets the topâband descriptors for aim and relevance, and strong theoretical linkage is evident. Minor imprecision in hypothesis operationalization and somewhat brief contextual detail justify a highâbutânotâperfect score of 5.

strengths: Research design (repeated measures) is described and well justified to control variability and order effects
Convenience sampling and participant characteristics are described with explicit rationale (English fluency, time efficiency)
Controlled variables and materials are listed and mostly justified, demonstrating reliability awareness
weaknesses: Materials description could further justify validity of word lists beyond replication
Coherence could be improved with a transition linking back to the aim
Some control details (lighting, screen distance) are omitted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is consistently in the top band: design, sampling and controls are not only described but also explained. Minor omissions and scope for clearer transitions do not detract from awarding full marks (4/4).

strengths: Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied appropriately
Interpretation links the statistical outcome directly to the Stroop effect hypothesis
Graph choice matches the research question and aids understanding
weaknesses: Standard deviation is mischaracterized as a measure of central tendency
Wilcoxon W value is reported incorrectly (negative) and exact pâvalue/effect size are missing
Graph lacks error bars, has truncated axis and incomplete labeling
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis meets the midâband: correct application of statistics and interpretation are present, but multiple technical and reporting errors significantly limit the score to 3 out of 6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with clear reference to selective attention, automaticity and speedâofâprocessing theories
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained and directly tied to the studyâs validity and generalizability
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations
weaknesses: Discussion remains surfaceâlevel without integration of contemporary empirical literature beyond Stroopâs original study
Modification details lack logistical specifics (e.g., participant numbers, assignment methods)
Generalization to the teaching profession is asserted without sufficient data support
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation consistently fits the top of the 3â4 band: relevant theory links, thoughtful strengths/limitations and justified modifications. Depth could be improved, so a score of 4 out of 6 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph comparing the two conditions is an appropriate technique and it clearly relates to the hypothesis.  Minor issuesâabsence of error bars, slightly truncated axis and a non-rounded mean (9.919)âconstitute errors, so the work fits the 3-4 descriptor; 3 marks reflect these shortcomings.

The student explicitly states that the null hypothesis is rejected because W < critical value and links this to the observed increase in naming time, tying the statistical outcome back to the hypothesis and data trend.  Interpretation is correct but concise and lacks effect-size commentary, so a 5 rather than 6 is awarded.

Both null and directional research hypotheses are presented.  The IV (congruent vs incongruent word lists) and the DV (time taken to name colours) are explicitly named and quantifiable, so they are operationalised.  Minor imprecision about timing procedures prevents a perfect score, so a 5 is appropriate.

Convenience sampling is identified, participant characteristics are given, and the rationale (time efficiency, need for English fluency) is explicitly stated.  This constitutes an explanation of both technique and choice, matching the 3-4 band; the coverage is solid, so the full 4 marks are awarded within the band.

Selective-attention theory, automaticity theory and speed-of-processing theory are described in accessible language and each is linked, at least briefly, to why incongruency should slow naming time.  Although the theoretical treatment is not very detailed, the link between theory and the planned investigation is clear and correct, warranting a score in the 5â6 band; a mid-band mark of 5 reflects some superficial explanations.

Findings are discussed with reference to selective attention, automaticity and speed-of-processing theories, but the discussion remains surface-level and does not integrate empirical literature beyond the original Stroop study.  This meets the 3-4 descriptor, and the clearer elements merit the top of that band.

The student states a precise aim â to see whether incongruent wordâcolour pairs increase naming time â and explicitly explains its relevance in terms of testing selective-attention capacity and replicating the Stroop effect.  The explanation makes clear why answering the aim matters for understanding attentional processes, so the descriptor for the top band is fully met.

Several strengths (repeated measures, counterbalancing, homogeneous education level) and limitations (practice effects, restricted sample) are stated and explained in relation to validity and generalisability, satisfying the descriptor for band 3-4.  Depth is adequate but not exhaustive, so the higher mark of 4 is warranted.

Several controls (colour-blindness check, identical word lists, noise level, stopwatch) and materials are listed and most are justified (e.g., same list increases reliability).  The explanations are rather brief and some controls (lighting, screen distance) are omitted, so a lower mark within the 3-4 band is appropriate.

Two clear modifications (switch to independent design; broaden and stratify the sample with a cognitive pre-test) are proposed and explicitly linked to identified limitations, giving justification.  Additional logistical detail is limited, so the strand fits the 3-4 band; a mid-band mark of 3 is appropriate.

The choice of a repeated-measures design is not only described but also explained in terms of controlling participant variability and order effects.  The explanation is clear and accurate, meeting the requirements of the upper band for this criterion.

Both descriptive (means, SDs) and inferential (Wilcoxon signed-ranks) statistics are applied.  However SD is erroneously described as a measure of central tendency, the W value is given as a negative number, and no exact p-value or effect size is reported.  These errors keep the work in the 3-4 band; a mid-band score of 3 is justified.

annotations.comment: The bar graph is appropriate but missing error bars, and axis labels are slightly truncated. Add complete labels and error bars to strengthen presentation.
Reporting W as negative contradicts the convention of summing absolute ranks; a positive W value should be presented. Also include exact p-value and effect size.

The introductory description defines the Stroop Effect clearly but lacks precise operational definitions and context for the independent and dependent variables. Consider integrating explicit variable definitions to strengthen clarity.
The explanation of selective attention, automaticity and speedâofâprocessing theories is clear and shows solid understanding of underlying models tied to the Stroop Effect phenomenon.

The description of the original 1935 study is brief and ends abruptly. Expand the methodological detailsâsample size, task specifics and timing proceduresâto provide a comprehensive context for your replication.
Detailed recounting of control, experimental and squareâcolor conditions along with percentage increases demonstrates thorough engagement with the original studyâs findings.
The investigation aim is clearly articulated and its relevance to selective attention processes is well justified, fully meeting the highest band for stating and explaining aim.
The null hypothesis names IV and DV but could operationalize timing procedure more precisely (e.g. start/stop criteria) to strengthen its clarity.
Consider restating the directional hypothesis using concise statistical notation, for example: $$H_1: \\mu_{incongruent} > \\mu_{congruent}$$ to improve precision.
A brief transition sentence would improve coherence when moving from introduction to exploration. Link back to the aim and state what will be described next.
Choice of a repeatedâmeasures design is well explained with clear rationale for controlling participant variability and order effects.
Convenience sampling and participant demographics are described with a clear rationale for English fluency and time efficiency.
The ethics section ends midâsentence, making it incomplete. Expand the description of debriefing and withdrawal procedures to ensure transparency.
Controlled variables are clearly listed and justified, demonstrating awareness of reliability concerns.
Materials description is thorough but could benefit from explaining validity of the word list beyond mere replication to justify selection.
Introduce the analysis section with an explicit statement of data checks (e.g. normality, outliers) before applying statistical tests for clarity.
Table 1 lacks units for mean and standard deviation headers. Include â(seconds)â and consider adding error bars to the corresponding graph.
Expand the table caption to indicate sample size (n=9) and mention the planned inferential test to provide full context.
The narrative misstates standard deviation as central tendency. SD measures dispersion; mean is the central tendency measure.
Interpretation effectively links statistical outcome to the Stroop Effect phenomenon, providing a clear conclusion about attentional processes.
Discussion references selective attention, speed-of-processing and automaticity theories, demonstrating integration of findings with background models.
Discussion remains surface-level without referencing empirical literature beyond Stroopâs original study. Incorporate recent research to deepen evaluation.
Strengths and limitations are directly linked to design choices and validity concerns, showing sound evaluation of methodological decisions.
Modification suggestion to switch to independent measures is identified but lacks detailed justification regarding participant numbers and random assignment logistics.
Evaluation thoughtfully discusses trade-offs between repeated and independent measures designs, indicating an understanding of design impact on reliability and variability.
The limitation concerning a teacher-only sample could be linked to specific biases (e.g. prior knowledge) and you might propose quotas by profession or age to improve generalizability.
Conclusion reiterates key findings effectively but should also address broader implications for attentional research and outline avenues for future studies.
The claim that results generalize specifically to the teaching profession is unsupported by data from a small, homogeneous sample; temper this or provide additional justification.
